summary:
The Good Friday Agreement (also known as the Belfast Agreement) was signed on 10 April 1998 and marked the formal end of the most intense phase of the Troubles — the ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that had claimed over 3,500 lives since the late 1960s. The agreement was reached after multi-party negotiations involving the British and Irish governments, the Ulster Unionist Party, the Social Democratic and Labour Party, Sinn Féin, and several smaller parties.

The agreement established power-sharing arrangements between unionists and nationalists in a devolved Northern Ireland Assembly, created new cross-border institutions linking Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, and outlined a framework for the decommissioning of paramilitary weapons. Both governments made constitutional amendments: the Republic of Ireland removed its territorial claim to Northern Ireland, while the UK reaffirmed Northern Ireland's right to self-determination. The agreement was endorsed by referendums in both Northern Ireland and the Republic.

The Good Friday Agreement is widely regarded as a model for conflict resolution, demonstrating how intractable ethno-national conflicts can be addressed through inclusive political negotiation. While implementation has been uneven and the power-sharing institutions have experienced periods of collapse, Northern Ireland has been largely peaceful since 1998. The agreement's provisions relating to the Irish border became a central issue in subsequent debates over Brexit.
